title: Why the Tax Code Was Actually Written to Help Women Entrepreneurs Win
guestBio: Barbara Schreihans is the founder of Your Tax Coach and a nationally recognized tax strategist. She specializes in helping entrepreneurs understand how to leverage the tax code legally to maximize their wealth and minimize their tax burden.
excerpt: Tax strategist Barbara Schreihans reveals why only 12 pages of the 7,000-page tax code tell you what to pay, and how women entrepreneurs can use the remaining pages to their advantage. This conversation will completely transform how you think about taxes and money.
metaDescription: Tax strategist Barbara Schreihans shares how women entrepreneurs can legally reduce taxes, choose the right business entity, and build wealth.
keyTakeaways: Only 12 pages of the 7,000-page tax code actually tell you what taxes to pay—the rest shows you how to legally reduce them.
Choosing the wrong business entity structure can cost you 15.3% in unnecessary self-employment taxes.
S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited compared to other business structures.
Women consistently outperform men in investment returns but tend to invest less money overall.
Having regular 'money dates' with your partner can strengthen your relationship while building wealth.
You can start investing with as little as $5 per week and build substantial wealth over time.
Understanding proper tax write-offs for travel, meals, and vehicles can save thousands annually.

pullQuotes.text: The tax code was written for you—it's time to use it.

content:
What if everything you believed about taxes was wrong? What if, instead of being designed to punish successful women, the tax code was actually written to help you build wealth and keep more of what you earn?

This paradigm-shifting perspective comes from Barbara Schreihans, founder of Your Tax Coach and nationally recognized tax strategist, who joined Aimee Rickabus on The Manage Her® to completely transform how women entrepreneurs think about taxes and money.

The Truth About the Tax Code That Changes Everything

Here's what will blow your mind: of the 7,000 pages that make up our tax code, only 12 pages actually tell you what taxes you owe. The remaining 6,988 pages? They're essentially a roadmap showing you how to legally reduce your tax burden.

The tax code wasn't written to punish entrepreneurs—it was written to incentive the behaviors that drive economic growth. When you start your business, hire employees, invest in equipment, or contribute to retirement accounts, you're doing exactly what the government wants to encourage. And they reward you for it through tax reductions.

This reframe changes everything. Instead of viewing taxes as something that happens to you, you can start seeing them as something you have significant control over through strategic planning and smart business decisions.

The Business Structure Mistake Costing You Thousands

One of the most expensive mistakes Barbara sees women entrepreneurs make is operating as a sole proprietorship when they should have elected S-Corp status. This single decision can cost you 15.3% in unnecessary self-employment taxes on every dollar of profit your business generates.

When you operate as a sole proprietor, you pay self-employment tax on your entire business profit. But with S-Corp election, you only pay self-employment tax on the reasonable salary you pay yourself, not on the distributions you take from business profits. For a business generating $100,000 in annual profit, this difference could save you over $15,000 per year.

Even better? S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited compared to other business structures. The IRS views S-Corps as more sophisticated and compliant, which translates to fewer red flags and less scrutiny.

Maximizing Deductions the Right Way

Barbara emphasizes that aggressive doesn't mean illegal when it comes to tax strategy. The key is understanding what expenses are legitimately deductible and documenting them properly.

Travel expenses become deductible when there's a genuine business purpose. That family vacation can include deductible elements if you're meeting with clients, scouting locations, or conducting other business activities. The key is having clear business intent and proper documentation.

Vehicle expenses offer another significant opportunity. Whether you choose actual expense method or standard mileage rate, tracking your business use consistently can generate thousands in annual deductions. The same principle applies to home office expenses, business meals, and professional development costs.

Building Wealth Beyond Tax Savings

While tax strategy is crucial, Barbara stresses that the ultimate goal is building long-term wealth. This means not just keeping more of what you earn, but making that money work harder for you through smart investing.

Women have a documented advantage in investing—studies consistently show that women outperform men in investment returns. Women tend to be more patient, less likely to panic sell during market downturns, and more focused on long-term goals rather than short-term gains. However, women also tend to invest less money overall, which limits the compound growth potential.

The solution isn't complicated: start where you are, with what you have. Barbara advocates beginning with just $5 per week if that's what your budget allows. The habit of consistent investing matters more initially than the amount. As your income grows, you can increase your investment contributions accordingly.

The Power of Money Conversations

For women in relationships, Barbara introduces the concept of regular "money dates" with your partner. These aren't stressful budget meetings, but rather intentional conversations about your financial goals, dreams, and strategies.

During these conversations, you review your progress toward goals, discuss upcoming financial decisions, and ensure you're both aligned on priorities. This approach transforms money from a source of relationship stress into a tool for building intimacy and shared purpose.

Many couples avoid money conversations because they're afraid of conflict or judgment. But Barbara's experience shows that couples who talk openly about money actually grow closer and build wealth faster than those who avoid the topic.

Retirement Planning That Actually Works

Traditional retirement advice often falls short for entrepreneurs because it's designed for employees with steady paychecks and employer 401k matches. As a business owner, you have access to more powerful retirement savings vehicles that can shelter significantly more income from taxes.

SEP-IRAs, Solo 401ks, and defined benefit plans can allow you to contribute $50,000, $60,000, or even six-figure amounts annually to retirement accounts, depending on your business structure and income level. These contributions are typically fully deductible, meaning they reduce your current tax burden while building your future wealth.

Taking Action on What You've Learned

The most important takeaway from Barbara's expertise isn't any single strategy—it's the mindset shift from victim to victor when it comes to your finances. You have more control over your financial outcome than you realize, and the systems are actually designed to help you succeed.

Start by examining your current business structure. Are you leaving money on the table by operating as a sole proprietorship when S-Corp election would save you thousands? Next, look at your record-keeping systems. Are you tracking and documenting all legitimate business expenses?

Finally, commit to having those money conversations, whether with your partner or with qualified professionals who can help you optimize your strategy. The tax code was written for entrepreneurs like you—now it's time to use it to your advantage.
